title: OPPOSITION: BLACK BACCARA (003217042)
text:
OPPOSITION Nо B 3 217 042

Baccarat (Société Anonyme), rue des Cristalleries, 54120 Baccarat, France (opponent), represented by Cabinet Meyer & Partenaires, Espace Européen de l'Entreprise 2 rue de Dublin, 67300 Schiltigheim, France (professional representative) 

a g a i n s t

Yevgen Pershyn, Eichendorffstraße 76, 94315 Straubing, Germany (applicant), represented by Agency Tria Robit, Vilandes Iela 5-2, 1010 Riga, Latvia (professional representative).

On 19/03/2026, the Opposition Division takes the following

DECISION:

REASONS

On 13/05/2024, the opponent filed an opposition against all the goods (Class 3) of European Union trade mark application No 18 980 105  (figurative mark). The opposition is based on European Union trade mark registration No 13 429 361 ‘BACCARAT ROUGE 540’ (word mark). The opponent invoked Articles 8(1)(b) and 8(5) EUTMR.

PROOF OF USE

In accordance with Article 47(2) and (3) EUTMR, if the applicant so requests, the opponent must furnish proof that, during the five-year period preceding the date of filing or, where applicable, the date of priority of the contested trade mark, the earlier trade mark has been put to genuine use in the territories in which it is protected in connection with the goods or services for which it is registered and which the opponent cites as justification for its opposition, or that there are proper reasons for non-use. The earlier mark is subject to the use obligation if, at that date, it has been registered for at least five years.

The same provision states that, in the absence of such proof, the opposition will be rejected.

The applicant requested that the opponent submit proof of use of the trade mark on which the opposition is based, namely European Union trade mark registration No 13 429 361.

The request was submitted in due time and is admissible given that it was submitted as an unconditional request in a separate document and the earlier trade mark was registered more than five years prior to the relevant date mentioned above.

The date of filing of the contested application is 30/01/2024. The opponent was therefore required to prove that the trade marks on which the opposition is based was put to genuine use in the European Union from 30/01/2019 to 29/01/2024 inclusive.

Furthermore, the evidence must show use of the trade mark  for the goods on which the opposition is based, namely the following:

Class 3: Perfumery, cosmetics, creams, milks, lotions; gels and powders for the face, the body and the hands; skincare cosmetics, creams (cosmetic -), pomades for cosmetic purposes, lotions for cosmetic purposes; facial and body creams and lotions; non-medicated lotions; oils for cosmetic purposes, almond milk for cosmetic purposes; cosmetic kits, cotton wool for cosmetic purposes; sun protection preparations (cosmetic sun tanning preparations), sun-tanning preparations [cosmetics]; baths (cosmetic preparations for -), cosmetic kits, bath salts and gels, not for medical purposes; massage oils and gels, not for medical purposes; antiperspirants [toiletries]; deodorant soap, deodorants for personal use (perfumery); perfumery, room perfumes, perfume water, lotions (perfumery), flower perfumes (bases for -); eau de cologne, perfume water, eau de cologne, musk (perfumery), essential oils, essential oils, extracts of flowers (perfumes), oils for perfumes and scents, oils for toilet purposes, almond oils, jasmine oil, lavender oil, rose oil; cleansing milk for toilet purposes, cosmetic masks, skin whitening creams; make-up, make-up removing preparations, face powder, make-up, lipstick, mascara, eyebrow pencils, eyebrow pencils; nail polish, toiletries, colorants for toilet purposes, colorants for toilet purposes; soaps, soap, dentifrices, talcum powder, for toilet use; hair lotions, shampoo; shaving preparations, after-shave preparations; incense, scented wood; depilatories; cleaning, polishing, scouring and abrasive preparations.

According to Article 10(3) EUTMDR, the evidence of use must consist of indications concerning the place, time, extent and nature of use of the opposing trade mark for the goods or services in respect of which it is registered and on which the opposition is based.

On 20/02/2025, in accordance with Article 10(2) EUTMDR, the Office gave the opponent until 25/04/2025 to submit evidence of use of the earlier trade marks. On 24/04/2025, within the time limit, the opponent submitted evidence of use.

The evidence to be taken into account is the following:

	Annex 1: Press articles and reports referring to the use of ‘BACCARAT ROUGE 540’ in relation to perfume in well-known magazines such as ‘Harper’s Bazar’ and ‘Marie Claire’. The date, reach and MIV (estimated value of the visibility a magazine generates for a brand) are indicated by the opponent on top of each excerpt. No audited circulation report was provided. Some of the magazines are dated after the relevant imer period but most of them are dated within. ‘BACCARAT ROUGE 540’ is shown on bottle photos or mentioned in the text of the articles. An example is shown below:

	Annex 2: 35 screenshots from Baccarat's official social media accounts (Facebook, Instagram, X, YouTube, Pinterest). The screenshots show perfume bottles with ‘BACCARAT ROUGE 540’ imprinted on them. ‘BACCARAT ROUGE 540’ is also used in the text of the posts. The vast majority of posts shows a picture and the likes or views it received. The latter are in the lower four digit range, sometime in the three-digit range. No user engagement data was provided. Example screenshots are provided in the following:

	Annex 3: 40 screenshots from the social media accounts of Maison Francis Kurkdjian (Facebook, Instagram). The company Maison Francis Kurkdjian is the licensee of Baccarat. The screenshots show perfume bottles with ‘BACCARAT ROUGE 540’ imprinted on them. ‘BACCARAT ROUGE 540’ is also used in the text of the posts. The vast majority of posts shows a picture and the likes, views and comments it received. The latter are in the mid to lower five-digit range, sometime in the four-digit range. The comments are in the two-digit range. No user engagement data was provided.

	Annex 4: One newsletter dated 15/03/2023, sent from the opponent’s news account news@by.baccarat.com. It shows perfume bottles with ‘Maison Francis Kurkdjian’ ‘BACCARAT ROUGE 540’ imprinted on them. 

	Annex 5: Screenshots from Baccarat's official website. The download is dated 08/08/2023. The screenshots show perfume bottles with ‘BACCARAT ROUGE 540’ imprinted on them. Parts of them show how the bottles are offered in the online shop. Example screenshots are provided in the following:

	Annex 6: Photographs of perfume bottles and packaging with ‘BACCARAT ROUGE 540’ imprinted on them, undated.

	Annex 7: 14 invoices (1825549 and 1678945 are duplicated), dated in the years 2019 to 2024 (wit the last one dated 06/01/2024), issued to recipients located in France, Germany, Poland, Malta, Italy, Netherlands Romania, Cyprus. Products labelled ‘BACCARAT ROUGE 540’ are listed in these invoices, among others. The product references are indicated which correspond to the product references in Annex 8 (for instance 2810839 for ‘BACCARAT ROUGE 540 eau de parfum 70 ml’). The billed amounts for these products vary from several hundred Euros to low four-digit ranges.

	Annex 8: Excel sheet containing the total net sales in Euros in selected Member States of the European Union by item. The period indicated on the table spans from 30/01/2019 to 30/01/2024. The list contains product references which appear in the invoices in Annex 7. The items sold range from several hundreds to the mid-five-digit range up to the mid six digit range in one country.

	Annex 9: Screenshot from the opponent’s website showing sales tabs for perfume products labeled with ‘BACCARAT ROUGE 540’. The downloads are dated 23/04/2025.

The evidence submitted within the substantiation period in order to prove the reputation of the earlier mark (annexes 1-10 submitted on 26/09/2024) was filed before the expiry of the time limit set for the opponent to submit proof of use. Any evidence that has been submitted by the opponent at any time during the proceedings before the expiry of the time limit for providing proof of use, even before the applicant’s request for proof of use, has to be automatically taken into account when assessing proof of use. Therefore, the aforementioned evidence will be taken into account both for the purpose of the assessment of proof of use of the earlier mark. This evidence is the following (despite the fact that the opponent labelled all pieces of evidence as ‘annex’ and thus duplicated the numbering, for better distinction the Opposition Division will lable the following evidence as ‘attachments’):

	Attachment 1: an excerpt, in English, presumably from a book or a guide (as the title indicates ‘BACCARAT musée’, in English: ‘Baccarat museum’), dedicated to the history of the opponent and the trade mark ‘BACCARAT’. The Opposition Division notes that this piece of evidence is not dated, and its origins are unclear. Furthermore, the history as recounted in the excerpts ends at the end of the 1980s (the last dates indicated in the timeline are the years 1986/7), thus the document is presumably from an older date. Therefore, it has limited weight in proving the reputation of the trade marks in question.

	Attachment 2: Copy of the first trademark BACCARAT in France.

	Attachment 3: A list (undated and of unknown origin) showing the opponent’s points of sale in France. The list does not specify the categories of goods.

	Attachment 4: Decisions of courts in France as well as decisions of the Arbitration and Mediation Centre of the World Intellectual Property Organisation (WIPO) and of the Spanish INPI. In all decisions the authorities have accepted the reputation of the trade mark ‘BACCARAT’.

	Attachment 5: A list of the opponent's trade marks registered in the world owned by the opponent. The Opposition Division notes that the number and duration of registrations and applications of the BACCARAT trade marks in Europe is relevant, but in itself weakly indicative of the degree of recognition of the signs by the relevant public. The fact that the opponent has many trade mark registrations (and in many classes) may indirectly attest to an international circulation of the brand, including online, but cannot decisively prove a reputation.

	Attachment 6: An excerpt, in English, from a book titled 'BACCARAT, THE PERFUME BOTTLES' published in 1986, presenting the collaboration of the opponent with popular perfume creators.

	Attachments 7 and 8: Examples of articles from the press from the years 2015 to 2020 covering the launch of the Baccarat Rouge 540 perfume and its coverage in the press in general. They contain references to Baccarat products, among them advertisements with perfume bottles. The advertisements show the sign ‘Baccarat Rouge 540’, often on a perfume bottle depicted in pictures featured in the articles. Some of the articles only refer to Baccarat as company and not to trade mark use (for instance the article in ‘Stiletto’ of 2014 which reports on Baccarat’s history and products).

Most documents show advertisements that were placed in the specified magazines. The extracts thus show some examples of the opponent's advertising campaigns for the promotion of its perfume under the trade mark ‘Baccarat Rouge 540’ in Europe. These documents attest, to some albeit limited extent, to the marketing activities of the opponent in the general and specialist press (for example the French fashion magazines 'Marie Claire' and ‘Elle’ and its local editions in other European countries, and the design magazine ‘Ramp Style’). Nonetheless, none of the pieces of evidence contains any relevant information about the advertising expenditure invested in the advertising visible in the press extracts. Tehe opponent did indicate the circulation number on each magazine in Annex 8. However, certified circulation audits have not been submitted. Thus, it has not been proven by independent objective evidence how many consumers were reached by the selected publications (whose names are sometimes very well-known, however, the opponent has to submit evidence for the outlet’s reach among consumers on record, it is not sufficient to simply quote a well-known magazine).

	Attachments 9 and 11: Various extracts showing the presentation of the Baccarat Rouge 540 perfume bottle on social media. Most excerpts are dated or can be attributed to a specific year, such as 2017, 2018, 2021 or 2022. Some show quantifiable metrics of engagement such as ‘likes’ ‘followers’ and so on. The like figures shown in many Facebook pages are several hundred at most. The same applies to the excerpts from the opponent’s Instagram and Twitter accounts. Considering that Facebook and Instagram have billions of users figures in this region do not attest to awareness of the trade mark among the public, particularly if one takes into account that the relevant public in France, the opponent’s most important market, consists of around 50 to 60 million consumers. The excerpts from the social media platforms alone can therefore not even remotely prove reputation. Without further detailed data, the Opposition Division is incapable of gauging the extent of the reach of the opponent’ social media presence.

	Attachment 10: Wikipedia article on Francis Kurkdjian, the French perfumer who created the Baccarat Rouge 540 perfume. The annex also contains what appears to be a press release on the perfume Baccarat Rouge 540 issued by Francis Kurkdjian.

Translation

The applicant argues that the opponent did not submit translations of some of the evidence of use and that therefore this evidence should not be taken into consideration. However, the opponent is not under any obligation to translate the proof of use, unless it is specifically requested to do so by the Office (Article 10(6) EUTMDR, former Rule 22(6) EUTMIR in force before 01/10/2017). 

Taking into account the nature of the documents that have not been translated and are considered relevant for the present proceedings - namely press articles, website extracts, social media accounts, invoices and photographs of packaging - and their largely self-explanatory character, the Opposition Division considers that it is not necessary to request translations.

First, the invoices and the Excel table contained in Annex 8 can be readily understood without translating the individual terms appearing in those documents. Their structure, the numerical data they contain, and the visible references to the earlier mark make it possible to identify the relevant information concerning the commercialisation of the goods.

Second, the social media and website extracts are likewise sufficiently clear. The Opposition Division can assess the use of the earlier mark on the basis of the visible content, such as images displaying the mark and the associated indicators of engagement (for example, ‘likes’, ‘followers’, or similar metrics). Any relevant quantitative information can be evaluated directly from the numerical figures appearing on each post or page.

The same considerations apply to the press and magazine articles. In this respect, the relevant evidentiary value lies primarily in the fact that the perfumes bearing the earlier mark are visible in the accompanying images or that the mark itself is expressly mentioned in the text. It is therefore not necessary for the full textual content of the articles to be translated, as the essential element for the purposes of the assessment is the visible reference to the earlier mark rather than a detailed understanding of the entire text.

For the sake of completion, it is stated that, as regards the evidence for reputation, either the opponent submitted necessary translations or the evidence is sufficiently self-explanatory.

Assessment of the proof of use

Place and time

The documents show that the place of use is the European Union. This can be inferred from the language of the press articles in the magazines in Annex No 1, the location of the recipients of the invoices submitted as Annex No 7 (France, Germany, Netherlands and so on) and the countries indicated the sales list of Annex No 8. Furthermore, it can be reasonably assumed that at least a part of the users which accessed the opponent’s social media accounts (Annexes Nos 2 and 3) and website and received its newsletter (Annexes 4, 5 and 9) were located in the European Union even if the opponent did not provide concrete data of user engagement. It is true, as the applicant argues, that the posts can technically not be attributed to a specific territory. Nonetheless, it is highly unlikely that posts of a company based in Europe would not be accessed at all by consumers in the European Union. For a finding of use of a trademark, even a limited connection to the relevant territory may be sufficient. It may be true that European languages are also spoken outside of Europe, however, contrary to what the applicant requests, evidence cannot be discarded entirely because of this fact alone. Moreover, the opponent indicated in Annex 1 in which countries the magazines were circulated. While hand-added indications on evidence documents have less evidentiary value, they must be trusted, nonetheless. 

As regards the magazine excerpts provided which appeared in the UK, the following applies: on 01/02/2020, the UK withdrew from the EU subject to a transition period until 31/12/2020. During this transition period EU law remained applicable in the UK. Therefore, use in the UK prior to the end of the transition period constituted use ‘in the EU’. Consequently, the evidence relating to the UK and to a period prior to 01/01/2021 is relevant with a view to maintaining rights in the EU and will be taken into account. The evidence relating to the UK and to a period after 31/12/2020 cannot be taken into account to prove genuine use ‘in the EU’ (see Communication No 2/20 of the Executive Director of the Office of 10 September 2020 on the impact of the United Kingdom’s withdrawal from the European Union on certain aspects of the practice of the Office, Section V ‘Earlier rights in inter partes proceedings’). Thus, the magazine articles relating to the UK, which are dated 01/09/2022, 20/11/2022, 04/02/2024 and 01/04/2024, can indeed not be taken into account. 

Therefore, it can be concluded that the evidence relates to the relevant territory.

Most of the evidence is dated within the relevant period from 30/01/2019 to 29/01/2024. As regards the documents which are dated after the relevant time period (some of the articles in Annex No 1 dated later than January 2024), the following applies: Evidence referring to use made outside the relevant time frame is in general immaterial, unless it constitutes conclusive indirect proof that the mark must have also been put to genuine use during the relevant period. The Court held in this context that circumstances subsequent to the relevant point of time may make it possible to confirm or better assess the extent to which the trade mark was used during the relevant period and the real intentions of the proprietor during that time (27/01/2004, C-259/02, Laboratoire de la mer, EU:C:2004:50, § 31). The latter is the case in the present matter, as the press articles which are dated outside of the relevant time frame only confirm what was evidenced within that period. The rest of the evidence falls squarely within the relevant period. It is not correct, as the applicant argues, that the majority of the evidence is not dated. For instance, this is patently incorrect as regards the invoices and the social media post which are all dated and all fall within the relevant time period. The fact that the opponent itself dated the magazines in Annex 1 and the sales figures in Annex 8 somewhat weakens the evidentiary value of these documents. Nevertheless, they must still be taken into account, as information provided by the parties is presumed to be accurate unless there are specific reasons to doubt its reliability. The only document lacking any date is Annex 6. Contrary to the applicant’s contention, these documents are not irrelevant; as indicated above, they help confirm the extent to which the trade mark was used during the relevant period and may therefore be considered. 

Extent of use

As regards the extent of use, all the relevant facts and circumstances must be taken into account, including the nature of the relevant goods or services and the characteristics of the market concerned, the territorial extent of use, and its commercial volume, duration and frequency.

The assessment of genuine use entails a degree of interdependence between the factors taken into account. Thus, the fact that commercial volume achieved under the mark was not high may be offset by the fact that use of the mark was extensive or very regular, and vice versa. Likewise, the territorial scope of the use is only one of several factors to be taken into account, so that a limited territorial scope of use can be counteracted by a more significant volume or duration of use.

The General Court has held on numerous occasions that use of an EUTM in a single Member State, or even in a single city in a Member State of the European Union, is sufficient to satisfy the criterion of territorial scope (07/11/2019, T 380/18, INTAS / INDAS (fig) et al., EU:T:2019:782, § 81 and the case-law cited). In other words, whether an EUTM has been used in one Member State or several is irrelevant. What matters is the impact of use in the internal market and, more specifically, whether it is sufficient to maintain or create market share in that market for the goods and services covered by the mark and whether it contributes to a commercially relevant presence of the goods and services in that market. Whether that use results in actual commercial success is not relevant (07/11/2019, T 380/18, INTAS / INDAS (fig) et al., EU:T:2019:782, § 82).

This is because the purpose of the proof of use institution is not to assess commercial success or to review the economic strategy of an undertaking, nor is it intended to restrict trade-mark protection to the case where large-scale commercial use has been made of the marks (08/07/2004, T 203/02, Vitafruit, EU:T:2004:225, § 36 to 38). Use of the earlier mark need not always be quantitatively significant in order to be deemed genuine (11/03/2003, C 40/01, Minimax, EU:C:2003:145, § 39; 16/11/2011, T 308/06, Buffalo Milke, EU:T:2011:675, § 51). Even minimal use can therefore be sufficient to qualify as genuine, on condition that it is deemed to be justified, in the economic sector concerned, for the purpose of preserving or creating a market share for the goods or services protected by the mark (27/01/2004, C 259/02, Laboratoire de la mer, EU:C:2004:50, § 21).

The invoices submitted by the Opponent show sales of the relevant goods ranging from amounts of a few hundred euros to sums in the four-digit range and were issued to recipients in France and other countries of the European Union. While these figures are modest, they clearly demonstrate actual commercial transactions and therefore cannot be regarded as merely token use. Contrary to what the applicant argues, it is not relevant how many items the opponent actually sold in a given year. As mentioned, a de minimis rule for how high the turnover or the number of sold items of a given good must be, cannot be laid down. Use of the mark by a single client, which imports the products for which the mark is registered, can be sufficient to demonstrate that such use is genuine if it appears that the import operation has a genuine commercial justification for the proprietor of the mark (27/01/2004, C-259/02, Laboratoire de la mer, EU:C:2004:50, § 24 et seq.). In addition, invoices serve as examples, they do not have to corroborate the full amounts of sales as they are indicted in Annex 8. These invoices are sparse but not ambiguous and span over the whole relevant period of time which also shows consistency. Moreover, the applicant’s argument that the invoices only show product abbreviations does not prevent the Opposition Division from taking them into account, since an overall assessment of the evidence clearly shows which products are concerned (see the comments made as to the product references above). Lastly, while it is correct that the engagement numbers in the social media posts in Annexes 2 and 3 are comparatively low, as the applicant points out, it must be reiterated that even a lower engagement level can indicate an exposition of the earlier mark to consumers which is to be considered sufficient for proving use. 

And as mentioned, use of an EUTM in a single Member State, or even in a single city within a Member State, is sufficient to satisfy the requirement of territorial scope, the decisive factor being whether the use contributes to a commercially relevant presence in the internal market rather than the geographic breadth of that use (07/11/2019, T-380/18, INTAS / INDAS (fig) et al., EU:T:2019:782, § 81–82). The opponent’s evidence shows use in several Member States with a focus on France. Moreover, the purpose of the proof of use requirement is not to assess commercial success or to require large-scale exploitation of the mark (08/07/2004, T-203/02, Vitafruit, EU:T:2004:225, § 36–38). Accordingly, even relatively limited sales volumes may constitute genuine use where they are capable of maintaining or creating market share in the relevant sector (11/03/2003, C-40/01, Minimax, EU:C:2003:145, § 39; 27/01/2004, C-259/02, Laboratoire de la mer, EU:C:2004:50, § 21). In the present case, the invoices demonstrate genuine commercial exploitation of the earlier mark and therefore go beyond mere token use. In addition, Attachments 7 and 8 corroborate this exploitation. 

Of course, the smaller the commercial volume of the mark’s exploitation, the greater the need for the opposing party to submit additional evidence capable of dispelling any doubts as to the genuineness of that use (08/07/2004, T-334/01, Hipoviton, EU:T:2004:223, § 37). Nevertheless, in the Opposition Division’s view, and applying the interdependence principle, the remaining evidence on file demonstrates to a sufficient extent that the earlier mark was used continuously throughout the relevant period. 

As a result, documents filed, namely predominantly the invoices, provide the Opposition Division with sufficient information concerning the commercial volume, the territorial scope, the duration, and the frequency of use.

The evidence shows that the mark has been used in accordance with its function and as registered for part of the goods for which it is registered (see further below as to which goods specifically).

Nature of use

In the context of Article 10(3) EUTMDR (former Rule 22(3) EUTMIR, in force before 01/10/2017), the expression ‘nature of use’ includes evidence of use of the sign  in accordance with its function, of use of the mark as registered, or of a variation thereof according to Article 18(1), second subparagraph, point (a) EUTMR, and of its use for the goods and services for which it is registered.

According to Article 18(1), second subparagraph, point (a), EUTMR, the following will also constitute use within the meaning of paragraph 1: use of the European Union trade mark in a form differing in elements that do not alter the distinctive character of the mark in the form in which it was registered, regardless of whether or not the trade mark in the form as used is also registered in the name of the proprietor. When examining the use of an earlier registration for the purposes of Article 47(2) and (3) EUTMR, Article 18 may be applied by analogy to assess whether or not the use of the sign constitutes genuine use of the earlier mark as far as its nature is concerned.

In the present case, all pieces of the evidence show use of the earlier word mark ‘BACCARAT ROUGE 540’. In addition, they show use of in the combination which the Opposition Division considers to be an acceptable variant. The slight stylisation, consisting of a font that imitates handwriting, still falls within what is commonly encountered in commercial signs and does not change the overall impression of the mark. Moreover, the use of ‘BACCARAT ROUGE 540’ in combination with  as standalone or in proximity to ‘Maison Francis Kurkdjian’ represents acceptable use as well as it is constitutes use of a mark in the same form as registered, in parallel with, but independently from, other marks (simultaneous use of independent marks). The signs are never shown as inseparable units but clearly different entities. 

In view of the above, the Opposition Division considers that the evidence shows use of the sign as registered within the meaning of Article 18(1), second subparagraph, point (a), EUTMR.

Summary

The Court of Justice has held that there is ‘genuine use’ of a mark where it is used in accordance with its essential function, which is to guarantee the identity of the origin of the goods or services for which it is registered, in order to create or preserve an outlet for those goods or services. Genuine use does not include token use for the sole purpose of preserving the rights conferred by the mark. Furthermore, the condition of genuine use of the mark requires that the mark, as protected in the relevant territory, be used publicly and outwardly (11/03/2003, C‑40/01, Minimax, EU:C:2003:145; 12/03/2003, T‑174/01, Silk Cocoon / COCOON, EU:T:2003:68).

Taking into account the evidence in its entirety, although the evidence submitted by the opponent is not particularly exhaustive, it does reach the minimum level necessary to establish genuine use of the earlier trade mark during the relevant period in the relevant territory.

However, the evidence filed by the opponent does not show genuine use of the trade mark for all the goods covered by the earlier trade mark.

According to Article 47(2) EUTMR, if the earlier trade mark has been used in relation to only some of the goods or services for which it is registered it will, for the purposes of the examination of the opposition, be deemed to be registered in respect only of those goods or services.

In the present case, the evidence shows genuine use of the trade mark for the following goods

Class 3: Perfumery.

Therefore, the Opposition Division will only consider the abovementioned goods in its further examination of the opposition.

LIKELIHOOD OF CONFUSION — ARTICLE 8(1)(b) EUTMR

Pursuant to Article 8(1)(b) EUTMR, a likelihood of confusion exists if there is a risk that the public might believe that the goods or services in question, under the assumption that they bear the marks in question, come from the same undertaking or, as the case may be, from economically linked undertakings. Whether a likelihood of confusion exists depends on the appreciation in a global assessment of several factors, which are interdependent. These factors include the similarity of the signs, the similarity of the goods and services, the distinctiveness of the earlier mark, the distinctive and dominant elements of the conflicting signs, and the relevant public.

a) The goods

The goods on which the opposition is based, and for which use was proven, are the following:

Class 3: Perfumery 

The contested goods are the following:

Class 3: Sanitary preparations being toiletries; Permanent waving and curling preparations; Cosmetic preparations for eyelashes; false eyelash kit, namely, lashes, tweezer, adhesive and sealer; Adhesives for affixing false eyebrows; Adhesives for affixing artificial eyelashes; Cosmetic preparations for eye lashes; Cosmetics for eye-brows; Artificial eyelashes; eyelash extensions; Wave-set lotions; Neutralizers for permanent waving; Fixative preparations for false eyelashes or eyelash extensions after cosmetic treatments; bonding agents for affixing false eyelashes and eyelash extensions; coating agents for artificial eyelashes and eyelash extensions; eyelash extension pads; Degreasing preparations for natural eyelashes; Preparations in gel form for dissolving adhesives for affixing eyelashes; Preparations in cream form for dissolving adhesives for affixing eyelashes; Cleaning preparations for natural and false eyelashes; Eyelash strips; Cosmetics for neutralising the odour of adhesives for affixing eyelashes; Stickers for protection against adhesives for affixing eyelashes; Cosmetics; Mascara; Long lash mascaras; Eye creams; Eye lotions; Eye sticks; Eye cosmetics; Eye makeup; Eyelid pencils; Eyebrow pencils; Eyelash tint; Eyelash dye; Eyebrow colors; Eyebrow gel; Eyebrow powder; Eyebrow mascara; Eye wrinkle lotions; Under-eye enhancers; Eye makeup remover; Gel eye masks; Eye brightening correctors; Double eyelid tapes; Self-adhesive false eyebrows; Eye compresses for cosmetic purposes; Eye care products, non-medicated; Gel eye patches for cosmetic purposes; Eyebrow colors in the form of pencils and powders; Cosmetics containing keratin; Artificial eyelash adhesive accelerators; Eyelash primer; Artificial eyelash glue holders; Eyelash extension conditioner; Eyelash extension mascara; Eyelash extension cleanser concentrates; Eyelash extension eyeliners.

The relevant factors relating to the comparison of the goods or services include, inter alia, their nature, their intended purpose, their method of use and whether they are in competition with each other or are complementary (‘the Canon criteria’). It is also necessary to take into account, besides the Canon criteria, other factors, namely distribution channels, the relevant public and the usual origin of the goods or services (02/06/2021, T‑177/20, Hispano Suiza / Hispano Suiza, EU:T:2021:312, § 21-22).

All the contested goods can be broadly grouped into the following categories, namely 

	cosmetic products for lashes,

	brows and eyes,

	artificial eyelashes and eyebrows,

	adhesives and bonding products,

	application accessories and tools,

	lash extension treatment and conditioning products,

	cleaning and adhesive-removal preparations,

	eye care products,

	permanent waving and lash treatment preparations,

	and other cosmetic preparations.

In short, these goods are cosmetic products, artificial lash and brow products and related application, treatment, care, and removal preparations and accessories.

These categories of goods belong to the market sector of cosmetics which is the same as that of the opponent’s perfumery. All the goods under comparison clearly belong to one homogeneous sector on the market and – at least – target the same relevant consumers, share the same distribution channels and usual commercial origin. Consequently, none of the contested goods can be considered dissimilar to those covered by the earlier mark. Although some of the goods under comparison could coincide in further relevant criteria such as purpose or method of use, it follows from the above considerations that all the contested goods are at least similar to a low degree to the opponent’s goods.

b) Relevant public — degree of attention

The average consumer of the category of products concerned is deemed to be reasonably well informed and reasonably observant and circumspect. It should also be borne in mind that the average consumer’s degree of attention is likely to vary according to the category of goods or services in question (22/06/1999, C-342/97, Lloyd Schuhfabrik, EU:C:1999:323, § 26).

In the present case, the goods found to be lowly similar target the public at large. The degree of attention is considered to be average.

c) The signs

The relevant territory is the European Union.

The global appreciation of the visual, aural or conceptual similarity of the marks in question must be based on the overall impression given by the marks, bearing in mind, in particular, their distinctive and dominant components (11/11/1997, C‑251/95, Sabèl, EU:C:1997:528, § 23).

The unitary character of the European Union trade mark means that an earlier European Union trade mark can be relied on in opposition proceedings against any application for registration of a European Union trade mark that would adversely affect the protection of the first mark, even if only in relation to the perception of consumers in part of the European Union (18/09/2008, C-514/06 P, Armafoam, EU:C:2008:511, § 57). Therefore, a likelihood of confusion for only part of the relevant public of the European Union is sufficient to reject the contested application.

The verbal elements ‘BACCARAT’ of the earlier mark and ‘BACCARA’ of the contested sign are likely to be understood by at least a part of the public as references to a casino card game of chance in the relevant territory. The spelling of the term varies slightly from territory to territory. For instance, in German the term can be spelled in three different variations: ‘Baccara’, ‘Baccarat’, ‘Bakkarat’. In most Western European languages, the card game baccarat is either identical or very close to the French/Italian name, often with only spelling or accent changes. Across the rest of the EU, the game name is almost always just a phonetic/orthographic adaptation of ‘baccarat’, typically treated as a loanword. Therefore, the part of the relevant public in the EU, which will recognise the reference to the card game, will perceive both ‘BACCARAT’ and ‘BACCARA’ as one and the same term. Nonetheless, given that there are no explicit gaming or casino-related references in the marks at hand or in the lists of goods under comparison, it must be assumed that at least a (bigger) part of the relevant public is unlikely to make an immediate connection with ‘Baccara’ as game when she or he is confronted with cosmetics. Thus, it must be assumed that at least a part of the relevant public perceives the elements ‘BACCARAT’ and ‘BACCARA’ as artificial terms without any meaning.

If understood as reference to the card game, the verbal elements ‘BACCARAT’ and ‘BACCARA’ do not allude to any of the characteristics of the goods at hand and do not possess a laudatory character and are thus normally distinctive. The same applies if the public does not make the association with the game as in this scenario both terms do not have any meaning at all.

For the sake of completeness and since this was debated by the parties, the Opposition Division mentions that a number of French dictionaries refer to ‘BACCARAT’ as ‘crystal object from the Baccarat factory’. However, these references merely confirm that this term is a trade mark of Baccarat, they do not prove that the public in France (or in another part of the relevant territory) attributes a meaning to the term ‘Baccarat’. Moreover, an entry like this does not prove either that the relevant public would perceive ‘Baccarat’ as a term which designates a place associated with the goods and services nor does it show that the term is well known for crystal. The fact that the earlier mark might be reputed for the manufacturing of crystal objects does not confer any meaning to the term ‘Baccarat’. 

Moreover, contrary to what the applicant argues, the fact that the expression ‘Black Baccarat’ is used for a hybrid type of rose is not known to a wider public but only to a small group of botanic specialists. A reference to a Wikipedia article on this type of rose does not prove that the wider relevant public is familiar with this meaning. It also remains unclear why, as the applicant argues, consumers would, on the one hand, perceive the contested sign as a reference to a botanical product and, on the other hand, the earlier mark as reference to glass crystal products even though the elements ‘BACCARAT’ and ‘BACCARA’ are nearly identical. The applicant’s arguments must therefore be dismissed.

In the context of the goods at issue, namely cosmetics, the elements ‘BLACK’ of the contested sign and ‘ROUGE’ of the earlier mark will be readily understood by the relevant public as references to colours. The English term ‘BLACK’ directly denotes the colour black. The term ‘ROUGE’, although originating from French, is widely understood across the European Union in the cosmetics field as referring to a red colour, in particular in relation to make-up products such as lipstick or blush. For cosmetics, colour indications are commonly used to designate the shade or appearance of the product (for example, black mascara, eyeliner, or red lipstick or the packaging of perfumes). Consequently, both terms are liable to be perceived primarily as descriptive or at least highly allusive indications of possible product shades rather than as indicators of commercial origin. In this context, the relevant public will attribute only a weak distinctive character to the elements ‘BLACK’ and ‘ROUGE’. Their capacity to distinguish the commercial origin of the goods is limited, since consumers are accustomed to encountering such colour references in the cosmetics sector as information about the characteristics or shades of the products. Therefore, the elements ‘BLACK’ and ‘ROUGE’ must be considered weakly distinctive in relation to the goods at issue.

As far as the public is concerned, which associates the elements ‘BACCARAT’ and ‘BACCARA’ with the card game, the following considerations apply: In the context of the casino card game Baccarat, the colours red and black are not merely generic colour indications but are conventionally associated with the two betting options ‘Banker’ and ‘Player’ as represented on scoreboards and tracking sheets. In this gaming context, ‘BLACK’ and ‘ROUGE’ (red) therefore evoke, for the relevant public familiar with the card game, a direct reference to opposing betting sides or outcomes, rather than colours in the abstract. In that case, the elements ‘BLACK’ and ‘ROUGE’ might not be allusive but are still adjectives subordinated to the elements ‘BACCARAT’ and ‘BACCARA’. Their role in the overall impression of both signs is still diminished. 

However, as explained above, the average consumer of cosmetics is unlikely to make an immediate connection with Baccarat, and will instead understand ‘BLACK’ and ‘ROUGE’ primarily as indications of colour or shade of make-up products.

The element ‘540’ of the earlier mark is normally distinctive. The concept of a number is the number it identifies, unless it suggests another concept such as a specific year which is not the case in the present matter. Thus, the element ‘540’ is normally distinctive. Nevertheless, and as already mentioned, ‘ROUGE 540’, with ‘ROUGE’ being an adjective, will be perceived as a qualifier to ‘BACCARAT’ which stands at the beginning where consumers tend to focus their attention. Consequently, this element as a whole has lesser weight in the overall impression of the earlier mark. As also already mentioned, the same considerations apply to the element ‘BLACK’ which is also an adjective, thus an identifier to ‘BACCARA’ and thus subordinated to the latter and consequently less relevant in the overall impression of the contested sign.

The stylisation of the verbal elements ‘BLACK BACCARA’ in the contested sign, which is nothing more than a standard bold font, will be perceived by the public as performing an essentially decorative function and will therefore not impact the overall impression of the contested sign. By contrast, the stylised rose that forms the letter ‘A’ of ‘BLACK’ is of a certain complexity and does not allude to any of the goods at hand. Therefore, it possesses a normal distinctiveness. Nonetheless, when signs consist of both verbal and figurative components, in principle, the verbal component of the sign usually has a stronger impact on the consumer than the figurative component. This is because the public does not tend to analyse signs and will more easily refer to the signs in question by their verbal element than by describing their figurative elements (14/07/2005, T-312/03, Selenium-Ace, EU:T:2005:289, § 37).

Visually, the signs coincide in the sequence of letters ‘BACCARA*’. They differ in the letter ‘T’ at the end of the verbal element ‘BACCARAT’. The elements share a significant number of letters (seven out of eight letters in each verbal element) with the first seven letters in the same position (and with same vowels), and none of them are strongly stylised. They coincide in their beginnings, to which consumers normally pay more attention when encountering trade marks, while the differences are confined to the endings (which applies to the letter ‘T’ which will thus not catch the public’s attention). The element ‘ROUGE 540’ of the earlier mark attracts less the public’s attention for the reasons mentioned above. The most striking difference stems from the contested mark’s ‘BLACK’ which, however, has a secondary role, too, for the reasons set out above. Nevertheless, the signs’ verbal elements (discounting the number ‘540’) are of comparable length.

On balance, the signs are visually similar to an above average degree.

Aurally, the pronunciation of the signs coincides in the sounds of the letters ‘BACCARA*’, present identically in both signs. The pronunciation differs in the sounds of the letter ‘T’ of the verbal element ‘BACCARAT’ of the earlier mark, which, however, does not affect the rhythm and intonation when these specific elements are pronounced. In fact, the French-speaking public does not vocalize the ‘T’ at the end of ‘BACCARAT’ due to the pronunciation rules of the French language and thus pronounces these verbal elements even identically. The pronunciation of the elements ‘BLACK’ and ‘ROUGE 540’ do distinguish the signs aurally as these elements cannot be found in the respective other mark. These elements are, however, secondary for the reasons outlined above.

In fact, the elements ‘BLACK’ and ‘ROUGE 540’, due their secondary position in the signs and/or weak distinctiveness, at least for part of the relevant public, are not likely to be pronounced by the public. In this regard, the Court has stated that elements that have a descriptive nature or are superfluous because of the nature of the goods and services are generally not pronounced (03/07/2013, T-206/12, LIBERTE american blend, EU:T:2013:342, § 43; 03/06/2015, T-544/12, PENSA PHARMA, EU:T:2015:355; 03/06/2015, T-546/12, pensa, EU:T:2015:355, § 107). 

As the pronunciation of the most distinctive verbal elements of the signs coincides in almost all letters, the signs are aurally similar to an above average degree or, if ‘BLACK’ and ‘ROUGE 540’ are not pronounced, highly similar.

Conceptually, reference is made to the previous assertions concerning the semantic content conveyed by the marks. For one part of the public, the signs refer to different colour shades, and only one contains the number ‘540’. As for this public the signs will be associated with dissimilar meanings, the signs are not conceptually similar.

For the other part of the public, both signs will be perceived as reference to a well-known card game. Considering that the colour references stem from weak elements, the only element conceptually distinguishing the signs to a larger extent is the number ‘540’ of the earlier mark. Nevertheless, the combination of ‘BACCARAT’ and the number ‘ROUGE 540’ does not create a new meaning which would render the signs dissimilar. As mentioned, above, ‘ROUGE 540’ functions as a mere qualifier to ‘BACCARAT’. Since the signs will be associated with a similar meaning, the signs are conceptually similar to an average degree.

As the signs have been found similar in at least one aspect of the comparison, the examination of likelihood of confusion will proceed.

d) Distinctiveness of the earlier mark

The distinctiveness of the earlier mark is one of the factors to be taken into account in the global assessment of likelihood of confusion.

According to the opponent, the earlier trade mark enjoys a high degree of distinctiveness as result of its long standing and intensive use in the European Union in connection with perfumery. This claim must be properly considered given that the distinctiveness of the earlier trade mark must be taken into account in the assessment of likelihood of confusion. Indeed, the more distinctive the earlier mark, the greater will be the likelihood of confusion, and therefore marks with a highly distinctive character because of the recognition they possess on the market, enjoy broader protection than marks with a less distinctive character (29/09/1998, C-39/97, Canon, EU:C:1998:442, § 18).

The opponent submitted the following evidence, which was already listed above and will be lister here once again for reasons of convenience:

	Attachment 1: an excerpt, in English, presumably from a book or a guide (as the title indicates ‘BACCARAT musée’, in English: ‘Baccarat museum’), dedicated to the history of the opponent and the trade mark ‘BACCARAT’. The Opposition Division notes that this piece of evidence is not dated, and its origins are unclear. Furthermore, the history as recounted in the excerpts ends at the end of the 1980s (the last dates indicated in the timeline are the years 1986/7), thus the document is presumably from an older date. Therefore, it has limited weight in proving the reputation of the trade marks in question.

	Attachment 2: Copy of the first trademark BACCARAT in France.

	Attachment 3: A list (undated and of unknown origin) showing the opponent’s points of sale in France. The list does not specify the categories of goods.

	Attachment 4: Decisions of courts in France as well as decisions of the Arbitration and Mediation Centre of the World Intellectual Property Organisation (WIPO) and of the Spanish INPI. In all decisions the authorities have accepted the reputation of the trade mark ‘BACCARAT’.

	Attachment 5: A list of the opponent's trade marks registered in the world owned by the opponent. The Opposition Division notes that the number and duration of registrations and applications of the BACCARAT trade marks in Europe is relevant, but in itself weakly indicative of the degree of recognition of the signs by the relevant public. The fact that the opponent has many trade mark registrations (and in many classes) may indirectly attest to an international circulation of the brand, including online, but cannot decisively prove a reputation.

	Attachment 6: An excerpt, in English, from a book titled 'BACCARAT, THE PERFUME BOTTLES' published in 1986, presenting the collaboration of the opponent with popular perfume creators.

	Attachments 7 and 8: Examples of articles from the press from the years 2015 to 2020 covering the launch of the Baccarat Rouge 540 perfume and its coverage in the press in general. They contain references to Baccarat products, among them advertisements with perfume bottles. The advertisements show the sign ‘Baccarat Rouge 540’, often on a perfume bottle depicted in pictures featured in the articles. Some of the articles only refer to Baccarat as company and not to trade mark use (for instance the article in ‘Stiletto’ of 2014 which reports on Baccarat’s history and products).

Most documents show advertisements that were placed in the specified magazines. The extracts thus show some examples of the opponent's advertising campaigns for the promotion of its perfume under the trade mark ‘Baccarat Rouge 540’ in Europe. These documents attest, to some albeit limited extent, to the marketing activities of the opponent in the general and specialist press (for example the French fashion magazines 'Marie Claire' and ‘Elle’ and its local editions in other European countries, and the design magazine ‘Ramp Style’). Nonetheless, none of the pieces of evidence contains any relevant information about the advertising expenditure invested in the advertising visible in the press extracts. Tehe opponent did indicate the circulation number on each magazine in Annex 8. However, certified circulation audits have not been submitted. Thus, it has not been proven by independent objective evidence how many consumers were reached by the selected publications (whose names are sometimes very well-known, however, the opponent has to submit evidence for the outlet’s reach among consumers on record, it is not sufficient to simply quote a well-known magazine).

	Attachments 9 and 11: Various extracts showing the presentation of the Baccarat Rouge 540 perfume bottle on social media. Most excerpts are dated or can be attributed to a specific year, such as 2017, 2018, 2021 or 2022. Some show quantifiable metrics of engagement such as ‘likes’ ‘followers’ and so on. The like figures shown in many Facebook pages are several hundred at most. The same applies to the excerpts from the opponent’s Instagram and Twitter accounts. Considering that Facebook and Instagram have billions of users figures in this region do not attest to awareness of the trade mark among the public, particularly if one takes into account that the relevant public in France, the opponent’s most important market, consists of around 50 to 60 million consumers. The excerpts from the social media platforms alone can therefore not even remotely prove reputation. Without further detailed data, the Opposition Division is incapable of gauging the extent of the reach of the opponent’ social media presence.

	Attachment 10: Wikipedia article on Francis Kurkdjian, the French perfumer who created the Baccarat Rouge 540 perfume. The annex also contains what appears to be a press release on the perfume Baccarat Rouge 540 issued by Francis Kurkdjian.

Belated evidence

On 24/04/2025, after expiry of the time limit to substantiate the earlier right and submit further material regarding its enhanced distinctiveness, the opponent submitted proof of use. The evidence to be taken into account is listed further above and reference is made to this list. 

Even though, according to Article 10(2) EUTMDR, the opponent has to submit evidence proving the earlier mark’s enhanced distinctiveness within a time limit set by the Office, this cannot be interpreted as automatically preventing additional evidence from being taken into account.

According to Article 10(7) EUTMDR, where the opponent submits after the expiry of the time limit set by the Office indications or evidence that supplement prior relevant indications or evidence submitted within the time limit, the Office may take into account the evidence submitted out of time as a result of an objective, reasonable exercise of the discretion conferred on it by Article 95(2) EUTMR. The Office must exercise its discretionary power if the late indications or evidence merely supplements, strengthens and clarifies the prior relevant evidence submitted within the time limit with the purpose of proving the same legal requirement laid down in Article 10(3) EUTMDR, namely, place, time, extent and nature of use of the opposing trade mark for the goods or services in respect of which it is registered and on which opposition is based.

When exercising its discretionary power, the Office must take into account, in particular, the stage of proceedings and whether the facts or evidence are, prima facie, likely to be relevant for the outcome of the case and whether there are valid reasons for the late submission of the facts or evidence.

In this regard, the Office considers that the opponent did submit relevant indications or evidence within the time limit initially set by the Office and, therefore, the later proof of use can be considered to be supplementary. Moreover, the fact that the applicant disputed the initial evidence submitted by the opponent in its submissions of 07/02/2025 justifies the submission of additional evidence in reply to the objection (29/09/2011, T‑415/09, FISHBONE / FISHBONE Beachwear (fig.), EU:T:2011:550, § 30, 33; 18/07/2013, C‑621/11 P, FISHBONE / FISHBONE Beachwear (fig.), EU:C:2013:484, § 36). This considerations holds even if it was the opponent’s intention to submit evidence showing the earlier mark’s use when it submitted the additional evidence. As evidence for use can also be evidence showing the earlier mark’s enhanced distinctiveness, its relevance can be assessed in this context as well. 

The additional pieces of evidence merely strengthen and clarify the evidence submitted initially, as they do not introduce new elements of evidence but merely enhance the conclusiveness of the evidence submitted within the time limit.

For the above reasons, and in the exercise of its discretion pursuant to Article 95(2) EUTMR, the Office therefore decides to take into account the additional evidence submitted on 24/04/2025.

Assessment of the evidence

The Opposition Division finds that the evidence submitted by the opponent does not demonstrate that the earlier trade marks have acquired a reputation through use.

In principle, the more indications the evidence gives about the various factors from which reputation may be inferred, the more relevant and conclusive it is for the proceedings. In the present case, as a first point, the evidence as a whole gives little or no quantitative data. Although the Opposition Division acknowledges that the evidence related to promotional activities (the magazine excerpts submitted as Attachments 7 and 8) may serve as an indication that the marks have acquired a reputation among the potential and actual purchasers of the goods in question, promotional activities are not sufficient on their own to establish that the earlier marks have indeed acquired a reputation. In particular, it is difficult to prove knowledge amongst a significant part of the public exclusively by reference to promotion or advertising carried out by the opponent, since the actual impact of the publicity on the perception of the public is difficult to measure. The opponent has provided some information on sales by way of the invoices in Annex 7, however, while this evidence suffices to satisfy the lower threshold of proving use, it is not sufficient to prove reputation which poses a far higher evidentiary threshold. And the opponent did not submit any further financial data contained in independently verified documents.

Furthermore, the opponent has not provided any information on the circulation of the cited magazines, save the indications which were noted by the opponent itself on the excerpts. While this makes it possible to assess their reach in the relevant public, as mentioned, the evidentiary value of such indications without objective auditing is low. Based on the fact that some of the magazines are well-known, the Opposition Division may assume that they reached a considerable audience, but this remains a conjecture on which the Opposition Division cannot base its assessment of the evidence as far as reputation is concerned. The reach of the publications and the advertising contained therein must be shown conclusively with evidence to a much greater extent that it is required for proving use, which the opponent has failed to do.

The Opposition Division stresses that reputation is not only and not even predominantly proven by showing sales but the Opposition Division must be in the position to gauge the opponent’s market share which as most relevant point to reputation.

The table in Annex 8 is as simple table made by the opponent itself whose accuracy cannot be verified in any manner. The probative value of this piece of evidence is low, however impressive its content may seem. The table submitted by the opponent as Exhibit 5 is also of little probative value, as it is a simple table prepared by the opponent himself. Moreover, it is unclear to what extent 15 points of sale in France can prove that a large part of the relevant public is familiar with the earlier marks. Many companies have a similar number of points of sale. Even when combined with the rest of the evidence, this is not very conclusive.

As matter of principle, and as already explained, for the purpose of proving reputation, information deriving directly from the opponent is unlikely to be enough on its own, especially if it only consists of opinions and estimates instead of facts, or if it is of an unofficial character and lacks objective confirmation, as for example when the opponent submits internal memoranda or tables with data and figures of unknown origin. The more independent, reliable and well-informed the source of the information is, the higher the probative value of the evidence will be. When it comes to proof of use, mere invoices might suffice due to the fact that the requirements for proof of use are fairly low. However, this cannot be applied to reputation/enhanced distinctiveness. 

Other pieces of evidence lack probative value for similar reasons: The extracts from the opponent’s website and web shop, are not particularly convincing evidence for reputation, more so without any additional data such as which audience they reach, how many consumers and so on. The same applies to Attachment 3. A mere list of stores is no indication of market share even if the list is long. The evidence documents meant to show reputation also seem scattered and random. The probative value of all of these documents is low.

Some of the pieces of evidence are either very old and/or inconclusive, too: On the one hand, the collector’s guide in Annex 6 attests to the fact that the opponent’s BACCARAT branded perfume bottles must be renowned, otherwise the guide would not have been published. On the other hand, the guide is aimed at collectors of perfume bottles, a group of people that, according to all life experience, should not be very large (in particular, considering that the text in the book informs the reader that the collectors in question paid 5000 US dollar for a collectible perfume bottle). Considering that trade mark awareness depends on the portion of the relevant public which is familiar with the mark in question, this piece of evidence is not suited to prove this awareness. In addition, the fact that the guide dates from 1986 makes it almost worthless as evidence for today’s reputation of the earlier marks as reputation must be proven to exist close to the date of publication of the contested mark. While a long standing trade mark history does help (and the opponent has proven at least the likelihood for a long brand history) reputation must clearly and unequivocally be established in the recent period of time. This is because reputation can be lost or fade away over time and the Opposition Davion must be in the position to determine beyond doubt that the trade marks in question are presently well known to a wider audience. 

The same applies to the guide excerpt which contains the opponent’s history in Annex 1. From the content, it appears that the opponent has a 200-year history, which is impressive in itself, but since the guide stops in the eighties the content does not seem to refer to the last thirty-six years. The publication date of the guide cannot be verified. Moreover, it seems to come from a museum, i.e., an institution that does not reach a wider audience per se, since it can be assumed that only few people visit a company's museum, no matter how famous the brand may be. The Opposition Division is therefore unable to draw any conclusion re the reputation of the earlier marks form this piece of evidence. 

Therefore, the submitted evidence is not appropriate or sufficient to provide indications about vital factors, such as trade mark awareness, market share and intensity of use. The opponent has certainly proven some use of its trade marks but even for the purposes of proving use the opponent’s evidence would fall short.

It is reiterated that proving use is not enough to reach the threshold of enhanced distinctiveness. None of the documents refer to the recognition of the earlier trade marks by the relevant public, nor there is evidence about the market share of the opponent's goods (decision of 21/04/2010, R 1054/2007-4, 'mandarino', paragraph 61). As regards the evidence submitted as Attachments 9 to 11 and Annexes 2 and 3, i.e. the extracts showing the opponent’s presence on social media, the Opposition Division refers to its remarks made in the list of evidence further above. However, in this context, the comparatively low level of engagement reflected in the extracts militates against a finding of reputation. Although some degree of leniency may be afforded to social media evidence when assessing use, it is not possible to determine market share on the basis of such material.

The evidence suggests that the opponent has lasted as a company for 250 years. Yet, the evidence also shows that it has used the ‘BACCARAT ROUGE 540’ for a perfume for a much shorter length of time. It is to be expected that it must be easy for the opponent to present extensive figures for its turnover and market share and to submit up-to-date evidence of its alleged reputation, in particular surveys which, with such a long brand history and the scale of marketing for the perfume at hand which can be inferred from the evidence, should easily be able to prove the earlier marks’ reputation.

The opponent invokes previous national decisions to support its claim of reputation. However, it must be born in mind that, although national decisions are admissible evidence and may have evidentiary value, particularly as they originate from territories that are relevant for the opposition at hand, they do not have a binding effect on the Office since the Community trade mark regime is an autonomous system. 

Even though previous national decisions are not binding, their reasoning and outcome should be duly considered. 

In this context it must be borne in mind that records of successful enforcement of a mark against (dissimilar) goods or services are important because they may demonstrate that, at least in relation to other traders, there is acceptance of protection against the use for certain goods or services. Such records may consist of the successful prosecution of complaints outside the courts, such as the acceptance of cease and desist requests, delimitation agreements in trade mark cases, etc.

Nonetheless, in the case at hand, the previous cases referred to by the opponent are not relevant to the present proceedings. In particular: 

	Decision of the Court of Appeal of Nancy of February 1980 and decision of the Court of Appeal of Paris of 25/09/2009 are not applicable to the present case since they merely refer to the reputation as a fact which is undisputed by the parties (or well known to the Court). However, the Opposition Division cannot base its finding of reputation on that fact that a national court treats the reputation as well known fact.

The decisions also lack any indication about the conditions of law and facts on the basis of which they were taken. There might be differences between the substantive and procedural conditions applicable in national proceedings and those applied in opposition proceedings before the Office. First, there may be differences in how the requirement of reputation is defined or interpreted. Second, the weight the Office gives to the evidence is not necessarily the same as the weight given to it in national proceedings. Furthermore, national instances may be able to take into account ex officio facts known to them directly, whereas the Office may not.

In addition, the evidentiary value of decisions which are this old is greatly reduced, in particular of the decision of the year 1980. While there is no formal time limit on how far back reputation can be proven and as long standing history of a trade mark helps proving reputation (and can even strongly indicate it), in principle, reputation must be proven in the time period preceding the date of application of the contested mark. Pieces of evidence which are dated more than 40 years previously cannot prove reputation on their own.

	Decision of the Arbitration and Mediation Centre of the WIPO of 28/02/2006 in the dispute about the domain name <baccaratjewelry.com> is of little probative value, as the conditions of law and facts on the basis of which they were taken are not made clear. In the absence of these elements, it is difficult for the Opposition Division to assess the decision's relevance with a reasonable degree of certainty. 

	Decision of the Arbitration and Mediation Centre of the WIPO of 07/03/2005 in the dispute about the domain name <crystal-baccarat.com> is of little probative value, as the decision is based on the United States trade mark registration for the mark BACCARAT CRYSTAL. Moreover, the decision refers to this trade mark as 'well known worldwide', and this assessment is too vague to give any useful information for the present case. 

	The same may be concluded about the decision of the Arbitration and Mediation Centre of the WIPO of 30/05/2007 in the dispute about the domain name <bacaraa.com>, namely the decision refers vaguely to the company Baccarat S.A. as a major, and widely known, competitor in the crystal and crystal chandelier manufacturing business for at least 200 years.

	Refusal decisions of 21/12/1998, 01/10/2008, 16/02/2015, 08/07/2022, 10/01/2024 appeal decisions of 16/05/2006, 14/11/2008 all of the Spanish INPI, in which the opponent has prevailed in opposition proceedings based on its BACCARAT mark. These decisions acknowledge that the earlier mark has reputation. Again, these decisions also lack any indication about the conditions of law and facts on the basis of which they were taken. Reference is made to the comments above made as to the French decisions. The same considerations apply to these decisions issued by the Spanish INPI. Again, the evidentiary value of decisions which are this old is greatly reduced, and this applies to the decisions of 1998, 2008 and 2015. Two recent decisions do not outweigh this lack of more recent evidence. 

	Last but not least, all the decisions mentioned were 15 years ago or more and are thus in no way suitable to prove the reputation for the present time. In order to be able to take these decisions into account, the opponent would also have had to submit more recent decisions, but these are not available.

Consequently, the assessment of the distinctiveness of the earlier marks will rest on its distinctiveness per se. In the present case, the earlier trade marks as a whole have no meaning for any of the goods and services in question from the perspective of the public in the relevant territory. Therefore, the distinctiveness of the earlier marks must be seen as normal, despite the presence of one weak element in earlier mark No 1, as stated above in section c) of this decision.

e) Global assessment, other arguments and conclusion

Evaluating likelihood of confusion implies some interdependence between the relevant factors and, in particular, a similarity between the marks and between the goods or services. Therefore, a lesser degree of similarity between goods and services may be offset by a greater degree of similarity between the marks and vice versa (29/09/1998, C-39/97, Canon, EU:C:1998:442, § 17).

The marks in question are visually and aurally similar to an above average degree or aurally even highly similar. The conceptual comparison either yields dissimilar signs, or the signs are conceptually similar to an average degree. The goods are similar to a low degree and address the general public whose degree of attentiveness is considered to be average. The earlier marks enjoy a normal distinctiveness.

Account is taken of the fact that average consumers rarely have the chance to make a direct comparison between different marks but must trust in their imperfect recollection of them (22/06/1999, C‑342/97, Lloyd Schuhfabrik, EU:C:1999:323, § 26). Even consumers who pay a high degree of attention need to rely on their imperfect recollection of trade marks (21/11/2013, T‑443/12, ancotel, EU:T:2013:605, § 54).

Taking into account all factors relevant for the global appreciation, including the principle of imperfect recollection, the visual and aural similarities of the signs and the similarity of the goods, albeit it low, lead to a likelihood of confusion. As regards the goods, which have been found similar to a low degree, it is recalled that evaluating likelihood of confusion implies some interdependence between the relevant factors and, in particular, the similarity between the marks and between the goods or services. Therefore, a lesser degree of similarity between the goods and services may be offset by a greater degree of similarity between the marks and vice versa (29/09/1998, C 39/97, Canon, EU:C:1998:442, § 17). In the present case, the visual and aural similarity between the signs is sufficient to counteract the low degree of similarity between some of the goods. This also applies in the scenario in which the public will perceive a conceptual difference between the signs as the difference stems from weak or secondary element. Therefore, the conceptual distinction cannot offset the visual and aural similarity of the signs. Therefore, the Opposition Division finds that the abovementioned interdependence principle tips the balance towards the finding of likelihood of confusion in respect of these goods.

Considering all the above, there is a likelihood of confusion on the part of the public.

Therefore, the opposition is well founded on the basis of the opponent’s earlier marks. It follows that the contested trade mark must be rejected for all the contested goods.

Since the opposition is fully successful on the basis of the ground of Article 8(1)(b) EUTMR, there is no need to further examine the other grounds of the opposition, namely Article 8(5) EUTMR.

COSTS

According to Article 109(1) EUTMR, the losing party in opposition proceedings must bear the fees and costs incurred by the other party.

Since the applicant is the losing party, it must bear the opposition fee as well as the costs incurred by the opponent in the course of these proceedings.

According to Article 109(1) and (7) EUTMR and Article 18(1)(c)(i) EUTMIR, the costs to be paid to the opponent are the opposition fee and the costs of representation, which are to be fixed on the basis of the maximum rate set therein.

The Opposition Division

According to Article 67 EUTMR, any party adversely affected by this decision has a right to appeal against this decision. According to Article 68 EUTMR, notice of appeal must be filed in writing at the Office within two months of the date of notification of this decision. It must be filed in the language of the proceedings in which the decision subject to appeal was taken. Furthermore, a written statement of the grounds for appeal must be filed within four months of the same date. The notice of appeal will be deemed to have been filed only when the appeal fee of EUR 720 has been paid.
